_format_scope: composition + arrangement + performance + stylistic_figures. Excludes mixing/mastering/recording — user handles those via Mix Analyzer on the produced track.
_high_confidence_fields: stylistic_figures around the hypnotic riff and Homme baritone-led arrangement
_to_verify_fields: exact key area B minor vs C minor
exact bar count of intro, verses, choruses and bridge
Bassist identity for this track (post-Oliveri lineup variable)
time_signature: 4/4 throughout. No metric trickery — this is a straight-ahead mid-tempo robot-rock cycle.
key_area: B minor area / C minor area. Chromatic riff motion + Homme baritone-friendly tonal area. Some readings hear the chorus briefly suggesting relative major motion before returning to minor center. Defensible reading: minor pentatonic + chromatic neighbor tones, possibly with bVII / bVI shifts in chorus.
tuning_documented: Drop D (D-A-D-G-B-E low to high). Standard QOTSA tuning carried into the post-Oliveri era.

composition.structural_blueprint: riff_driven_intro (full-band riff statement establishes hypnotic foundation) -> verse_1 (Homme baritone vocal enters declamatory over the riff cycle) -> pre_chorus_density_build (slight intensification, harmonic broadening) -> chorus (hook expansion, vocal projects, possible secondary harmony) -> riff_return -> verse_2 -> pre_chorus -> chorus -> bridge_instrumental (brief riff variation or harmonic bridge — no full guitar solo, more textural) -> final_chorus -> outro_riff_tag. Total ~4:51 — mid-length for Lullabies. Continues robot-rock philosophy from Songs for the Deaf but in HOMME-CENTRIC ERA without Oliveri's punk-aggression contributions.

section_count_and_lengths._note: Bar counts approximate. Track follows mature Homme verse-pre-chorus-chorus template — more conventional structure than the Oliveri-era tracks.

composition.harmonic_motion: Minor-modal with chromatic riff motion + Homme suspended voicings. Tonic B/C minor with chord shifts to relative major or bVI in chorus area. Harmonic motion is more developed than the strictly-static First It Giveth — chorus introduces real chord change, not just textural variation.
composition.modal_choice: B minor / C minor Aeolian with chromatic neighbor tones. Phrygian-tinged inflections possible in riff. Chorus modal area widens slightly, possibly touching the relative major before returning.
composition.melodic_contour: Homme's verse vocal sits in declamatory baritone with phrases tracking metric accents. Chorus melody opens out — longer sustained notes, slight upward gestures on hook syllables, possible secondary harmony layering. The riff itself is a chromatic-tinged minor figure that drives the verse foundation; chorus broadens via fuller voicings.
composition.characteristic_riff_construction: Drop-D minor riff with chromatic motion + suspended voicings. Built for hypnotic repetition — micro-variations only across iterations. The riff is identical in pitch content every cycle; perceptual interest comes from accumulating layers and dynamic accents, not from melodic development. Continues robot-rock philosophy — the riff IS the song's foundation, not a launchpad for development.
composition.harmonic_pacing: Slow at chord level (chord changes mostly within chorus — verse stays on tonic), medium at riff cycle level. Pacing is generated by the verse-pre-chorus-chorus structure rather than constant harmonic motion.
composition.phrase_symmetry: Symmetric 4-bar / 8-bar / 16-bar phrasing. The conventional verse-pre-chorus-chorus structure is more symmetric than QOTSA's experimental tracks (e.g., compressed Medication or epic A Song for the Dead). Mature commercial-rock template applied to Homme's robot-rock vocabulary.

instrumentation_complete: Electric guitar drop-D (Josh Homme, tube amp with moderate distortion + heavy pedal chains per Joe Barresi production)
Possible double-tracked rhythm guitar for stereo width
Possible additional guitar layers for textural variety (Barresi's documented heavy-rock specialization)
Electric bass drop-D (Lullabies-era bassist — Alain Johannes documented as varying contributor)
Drum kit (Joey Castellano — post-Grohl-guest-era documented QOTSA drummer)
Possible secondary vocal harmony in chorus

section_instrumentation.intro: Full band riff statement — drums + guitar + bass at full density
section_instrumentation.verse: Riff continues, Homme baritone enters, layering minimal
section_instrumentation.pre_chorus: Slight density build — additional guitar layer or accent, drum intensification
section_instrumentation.chorus: Hook expansion with Homme vocal projection + possible secondary harmony, fuller chord voicings
section_instrumentation.bridge_instrumental: Brief textural shift — possibly thinned guitar, slight modal shift, no formal solo
section_instrumentation.outro: Final chorus + riff tag, possibly fading or cold-stopping

arrangement.vocal_layering_strategy: Lead vocal foregrounded with possible chorus harmony. Verse single-tracked declamatory; chorus may add a harmony line for hook emphasis. Less vocal-democracy variation than Songs for the Deaf — Homme-centric era means fewer guest vocalists, vocal stays predominantly Homme.
arrangement.dynamic_arc_overall: Conventional verse-build-chorus-release cycle. Verses mid-density; pre-chorus builds; chorus releases with maximum density; bridge breathes; final chorus peaks; outro tags. Less convulsive than Songs for the Deaf tracks because the structure is more conventionally built.

instrumental_role_assignment.guitar_role: Hypnotic riff engine + textural broadening in chorus. Continues robot-rock philosophy. Possible double-tracked or layered for stereo and tonal variety per Barresi's production.
instrumental_role_assignment.bass_role: Tight kick-lock + low-end foundation. Pick attack for rhythmic clarity. Adapted to Homme-centric compositional voice — less aggressive presence than Oliveri-era bass.
instrumental_role_assignment.drums_role: Groove foundation, less technical display than Grohl. Castellano plays for the song's hypnotic groove rather than for spotlight moments. Adapted to Homme's robot-rock philosophy.
instrumental_role_assignment.vocal_role: Homme-centric melodic baritone — narrative carrier and primary melodic voice. Without competing punk-aggression vocal contribution from Oliveri, vocal balance is dominantly Homme.

harmonic_density_per_section.chorus: High — full band + lead + possible backing vocal + fuller chord voicings

arrangement_anti_patterns_avoided: No conventional guitar solo (bridge is textural rather than virtuosic)
No extended jam outro (this track stays within conventional song length, unlike A Song for the Dead)

performance.tempo_feel_description: Mid-tempo heavy groove at ~113 BPM. Feels DRIVEN but unhurried — tempo allows hypnotic groove development without rushing. Performance lock to the riff is tight; the track's mesmeric quality comes from the band's commitment to the cycle rather than from individual virtuosity.

drum_style.performer: Joey Castellano (Lullabies to Paralyze drummer — post-Grohl-guest-era documented QOTSA drummer)
drum_style.feel: Robot-rock groove foundation — Castellano plays for the song's hypnotic groove rather than for technical display. Adapted to Homme's documented robot-rock philosophy.
techniques_documented: Kick + snare backbone driving 4/4 at ~113 BPM
Less technical complexity than Grohl's Songs for the Deaf drumming — different drummer, different stylistic priorities
Cymbal accents articulate transitions between verse/pre-chorus/chorus
Possible tom rolls or fill statements at major section boundaries
drum_style.groove_feel_alignment: Pocket-priority. Drumming serves the riff cycle's hypnotic identity rather than competing for foreground attention.
drum_style.fills_and_transitions: Minor fills mark verse-to-pre-chorus and pre-chorus-to-chorus boundaries. No extended drum feature spotlight — Castellano serves the song rather than featuring.

feel_per_section.intro_riff: Hypnotic riff statement, drop-D, moderate distortion, locked to drum grid
feel_per_section.verse: Continuous riff cycle supporting Homme vocal, micro-variations in attack and accent
feel_per_section.pre_chorus: Slight chord-voicing broadening, additional layer or accent
feel_per_section.chorus: Fuller chord voicings + possible double-tracked rhythm widening stereo image
feel_per_section.bridge_solo: Textural rather than virtuosic — possibly thinned guitar or modal-shift gesture, no formal solo

techniques_documented: Tube amps with moderate distortion + heavy pedal chains per Joe Barresi production
Riff-driven verses with chromatic motion + suspended voicings (Homme signature)
Picking precision required for hypnotic-groove feel
Possible double-tracked rhythm guitars for stereo width
Heavy-rock tonal palette informed by Barresi's documented Tool/Melvins/Kyuss-related experience
guitar_style.playing_style_versus_other_grunge: Robot-rock vocabulary at maturity — Homme's compositional voice without competing influences. Compared to grunge contemporaries the playing is more locked-to-grid and less expressive-melodic. Compared to earlier QOTSA tracks the chord palette is slightly more developed (chorus has real motion), reflecting Homme's compositional maturation.

vocal_delivery.performer: Josh Homme (lead vocal — post-Oliveri Homme-centric era)
vocal_delivery.verse_delivery: Baritone declamatory — phrases align with metric accents. Mid-register, slight grit on harder consonants. Continued from Songs for the Deaf approach with slight maturation.
vocal_delivery.chorus_delivery: Hook expansion with vocal projection + possible secondary harmony. Sustained melodic phrases over fuller chord voicings. Hook crystallizes the absurdist lyrical frame.
vocal_delivery.outro_delivery: Vocal tags or fades with the riff outro — possibly final phrase repeated with slight variations
documented_vocal_quirks: Homme's documented baritone-friendly compositional choices continued
Multiple takes per Joe Barresi production
Possible secondary vocal harmony (without Lanegan documented as featured guest on this track — post-Songs-for-the-Deaf vocal democracy reduced)
Absurdist lyrical content delivered with melodic restraint rather than aggressive declamation

performance_anti_patterns_avoided: No formal guitar solo (bridge is textural)

stylistic_figures._note: Production gestures and figures of style — the rises, drops, transitions, climaxes, special effects that define the track's compositional identity beyond pure pitch/rhythm content.

transitions_between_sections.description: Homme's voice simply enters on top of the riff cycle — no fill, no riser. Riff continues unchanged underneath.

Pre-chorus adds an additional guitar layer or accent + slight drum intensification, then chorus arrives with fuller chord voicings + possible backing vocal. The build is GRADUATED rather than sudden — three steps from verse to chorus.
Bridge is achieved by thinning guitar texture and possibly shifting modal area briefly. Not a full breakdown — still mid-density — but listener feels the breath.
Full band re-engages at chorus density, possibly with additional emphasis fill. Compositional payoff for the bridge breath.
Vocal drops out; riff tags continue for a few bars before track ends. Less extended than A Song for the Dead's outro but follows similar logic of letting riff identity close the track.

risers_and_builds.description: Pre-chorus accumulates additional guitar layers + drum intensification + possible backing vocal entry, building toward chorus downbeat. Build is graduated rather than synthesized — riser is band-played not effects-generated.

cutoffs_and_filter_moves.where: throughout — possible subtle filter motion on guitar or drums
cutoffs_and_filter_moves.description: Per Barresi's documented heavy-rock approach, possible subtle EQ/filter motion across sections — brightening or muting timbre without aggressive sweeps. Not a featured production gesture but possibly present.

drops_and_breakdowns.description: Bridge thins texture to provide breath but does not strip back to bare elements. Functions as a breakdown-LITE rather than full breakdown.

climax_moments.description: Final chorus is the densest moment — full band + lead vocal + possible backing harmony + fullest chord voicings. Conventional climax placement at the song's emotional peak.

special_effects_and_textural_devices.description: Track represents Homme's compositional voice dominant without Oliveri's punk-aggression contribution. Album represents primary-voice-without-competing-influence rather than band-as-before with substitute. Lineup change as compositional pivot.
Riff cycles for entire track with micro-variations only — continues QOTSA's documented robot-rock philosophy. The constancy is the hook. Mature application of robot-rock vocabulary.

Lyrical content carries Homme's documented absurdist songwriting sensibility — title 'Everybody Knows That You're Insane' indicates the absurdist frame. Vocal delivery is melodic-baritone restraint over the absurdist content, not aggressive declamation.

Joe Barresi's documented heavy-rock specialization (Tool, Melvins, Kyuss-related lineage) informs the sonic palette — heavier guitar tone variety than Eric Valentine's Songs for the Deaf approach. Same role-philosophy across producers but different sonic output.

the_moment_people_remember.primary: The hypnotic riff cycle — Homme's mature robot-rock vocabulary at sustained mid-tempo. Listeners hook onto the riff's constancy.
the_moment_people_remember.secondary: The absurdist title and lyrical frame delivered with Homme's restrained baritone — the contrast between melodic delivery and absurdist content is the song's character.
the_moment_people_remember.tertiary: The post-Oliveri arrangement balance — listeners familiar with Songs for the Deaf hear that this is QOTSA without Oliveri's aggressive contribution
